news_summary: Scientists and AI researchers are making groundbreaking advances in quantum physics, antimatter behavior, AI world models, and humanoid robot speed capabilities.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques et les chercheurs en intelligence artificielle réalisent des avancées révolutionnaires dans les domaines de la physique quantique, du comportement de l'antimatière, des modèles du monde en intelligence artificielle et des capacités de vitesse des robots humanoïdes.
news_summary_es: Los científicos e investigadores en inteligencia artificial están logrando avances revolucionarios en física cuántica, el comportamiento de la antimateria, los modelos del mundo en inteligencia artificial y la velocidad de los robots humanoides.

articles.title: Scientists catch antimatter “atom” acting like a wave for the first time
articles.summary: Quantum physics once shocked scientists by revealing that particles can behave like waves—and now, that strange behavior has been pushed even further. For the first time, researchers have observed wave-like interference in positronium, an exotic “atom” made of an electron and its antimatter partner, a positron. This breakthrough not only strengthens the weird reality of quantum mechanics but also opens the door to new experiments involving antimatter, including the possibility of testing how gravity affects it—something never directly measured before.
articles.content:
One of the defining breakthroughs that set quantum physics apart from classical physics was the realization that matter behaves very differently at extremely small scales. Among the most important discoveries was wave-particle duality, the idea that particles can also act like waves.

This concept became widely known through the double-slit experiment. When electrons were fired through two narrow openings, they produced a pattern of alternating light and dark bands on a detector. This pattern revealed that each electron behaved like a wave, with its quantum wave-function passing through both slits at once and interfering with itself. Scientists later confirmed this effect with neutrons, helium atoms, and even larger molecules, establishing matter-wave diffraction as a key principle of quantum mechanics. However, despite these advances, this phenomenon had not been directly observed in positronium. Positronium is a short-lived, two-body system made up of an electron and a positron bound together and orbiting a shared center of mass. Because both components have equal mass, researchers have long sought to understand how such a system would behave when forming a beam and undergoing diffraction.

First Observation of Positronium Wave Behavior

A research team from Tokyo University of Science, Japan, led by Professor Yasuyuki Nagashima and joined by Associate Professor Yugo Nagata and Dr. Riki Mikami, has now achieved that goal. They successfully demonstrated matter-wave diffraction in a beam of positronium. The beam used in their experiment had the necessary energy range and coherence to produce clear interference effects. Their results, published in Nature Communications, provide strong new evidence of wave-particle duality in an unusual system.

"Positronium is the simplest atom composed of equal-mass constituents, and until it self-annihilates, it behaves as a neutral atom in a vacuum. Now, for the first time, we have observed quantum interference of a positronium beam, which can pave the way for new research in fundamental physics using positronium," says Prof. Nagashima.

Creating a High-Quality Positronium Beam

The breakthrough relied on producing a highly controlled positronium beam. To do this, the researchers first generated negatively charged positronium ions. They then used a precisely timed laser pulse to remove an extra electron, resulting in a fast-moving, neutral, and coherent stream of positronium atoms.

This beam was directed toward a sheet of graphene. The spacing between atoms in the graphene closely matched the de Broglie wavelength of the positronium at the energies used in the experiment. As the positronium atoms passed through the two-to-three-layer graphene sheet, some of them made it through and were detected. The resulting measurements revealed a distinct diffraction pattern, confirming wave-like behavior.

Clear Diffraction Patterns and Quantum Behavior

Compared with earlier techniques, this method produces positronium beams with higher energies, reaching up to 3.3 keV. It also provides a narrower spread of energies and a more tightly directed beam. Conducting the experiment in an ultra-high vacuum kept the graphene surface clean, allowing the diffraction pattern to be observed more clearly.

The results showed that even though positronium consists of two particles, it behaves as a single quantum object. The electron and positron do not diffract separately but instead act together as one wave.

"This groundbreaking experimental milestone marks a major advance in fundamental physics. It not only demonstrates positronium's wave nature as a bound lepton-antilepton system (a system that behaves like a tiny atom) but also opens pathways for precision measurements involving positronium," says Dr. Nagata.

The team also investigated whether positronium would produce interference in the same way as a single particle like an electron. Their findings confirmed that it does, reinforcing the idea that it functions as a unified quantum entity.

Future Applications in Materials Science and Antimatter Research

In addition to confirming its quantum properties, positronium diffraction could lead to practical applications. Because positronium carries no electric charge, it may be useful for analyzing material surfaces without causing damage. This makes it especially valuable for studying insulators or magnetic materials that can interfere with charged particle beams.

Looking ahead, experiments involving positronium interference could also make it possible to test how antimatter responds to gravity. This remains an open question, as direct measurements have not yet been achieved, even for electrons.

About Professor Yasuyuki Nagashima from Tokyo University of Science

Dr. Yasuyuki Nagashima is a Professor in the Department of Physics at Tokyo University of Science, Japan, specializing in positron and positronium physics. His research focuses on the properties of negative ions of positronium and the positronium beam. He also studies positron annihilation-induced ion desorption from solid surfaces. In 2020, he received the Hiroshi Takuma Memorial Prize from the Matsuo Foundation. His laboratory conducts fundamental research on exotic particle-matter interactions while developing new positron-based experimental techniques for applied physics.

About Associate Professor Yugo Nagata from Tokyo University of Science

Dr. Yugo Nagata is an Associate Professor in the Department of Physics at Tokyo University of Science, Japan, specializing in positronium and atomic physics. In 2023, he received the Young Scientist Award of the Japanese Positron Science Society.

This work was supported by JSPS KAKENHI (Grants Nos. JP25H00620, JP21H04457, and JP17H01074).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum physics: the first observation of wave-like behavior in positronium, an antimatter atom. This discovery advances fundamental physics knowledge and opens new avenues for research, including antimatter gravity tests and materials science applications, which have broad scientific and technological implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe wave behavior in antimatter 'atom' for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum physics shows that tiny particles, like electrons, can behave like waves, not just solid objects. This strange idea was proven with electrons and other particles, but had never been seen in positronium, a rare system made of an electron and its antimatter partner, a positron.

**What happened** – A team from Tokyo University of Science led by Professor Yasuyuki Nagashima created a beam of positronium atoms and sent it through a thin sheet of graphene. They observed a pattern that only happens when something behaves like a wave. This was the first time scientists saw positronium act this way, proving it has wave-like properties.

**Impact** – This is important because positronium is made of two particles with equal mass, making it different from other atoms. Seeing its wave behavior confirms that even this exotic antimatter system follows quantum rules. It also means scientists can now study antimatter in new ways, such as testing how gravity affects it, something never directly measured before.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use this discovery to explore antimatter’s properties more deeply. They hope to use positronium beams to study materials without damaging them and to try experiments that could reveal how antimatter reacts to gravity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have shown that positronium, an antimatter atom, behaves like a wave, opening new paths for studying antimatter and quantum physics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques observent pour la première fois le comportement ondulatoire d'un « atome » d'antimatière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La physique quantique montre que les particules très petites, comme les électrons, peuvent se comporter comme des ondes, pas seulement comme des objets solides. Cette idée étrange a été prouvée avec des électrons et d'autres particules, mais jamais avec le positronium, un système rare composé d'un électron et de son partenaire antimatière, un positron.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe de l'Université des Sciences de Tokyo dirigée par le professeur Yasuyuki Nagashima a créé un faisceau d'atomes de positronium et l'a envoyé à travers une fine couche de graphène. Ils ont observé un motif qui n'apparaît que lorsque quelque chose se comporte comme une onde. C'est la première fois que les scientifiques voient le positronium agir ainsi, prouvant qu'il possède des propriétés ondulatoires.

**Impact** – Cela est important car le positronium est composé de deux particules de même masse, ce qui le rend différent des autres atomes. Voir son comportement ondulatoire confirme que même ce système d'antimatière exotique suit les règles quantiques. Cela signifie aussi que les scientifiques peuvent désormais étudier l'antimatière de nouvelles façons, par exemple en testant comment la gravité l'affecte, ce qui n'a jamais été mesuré directement.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser cette découverte pour explorer plus en profondeur les propriétés de l'antimatière. Ils espèrent utiliser des faisceaux de positronium pour étudier les matériaux sans les abîmer et tenter des expériences qui pourraient révéler comment l'antimatière réagit à la gravité.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont montré que le positronium, un atome d'antimatière, se comporte comme une onde, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier l'antimatière et la physique quantique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan por primera vez el comportamiento ondulatorio de un 'átomo' de antimateria
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La física cuántica muestra que las partículas muy pequeñas, como los electrones, pueden comportarse como ondas, no solo como objetos sólidos. Esta idea extraña se ha demostrado con electrones y otras partículas, pero nunca con el positronio, un sistema raro formado por un electrón y su pareja de antimateria, un positrón.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad de Ciencias de Tokio dirigido por el profesor Yasuyuki Nagashima creó un haz de átomos de positronio y lo envió a través de una delgada lámina de grafeno. Observaron un patrón que solo ocurre cuando algo se comporta como una onda. Esta fue la primera vez que los científicos vieron al positronio actuar de esta manera, demostrando que tiene propiedades ondulatorias.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque el positronio está formado por dos partículas de igual masa, lo que lo hace diferente de otros átomos. Ver su comportamiento ondulatorio confirma que incluso este sistema exótico de antimateria sigue las reglas cuánticas. También significa que los científicos ahora pueden estudiar la antimateria de nuevas formas, como probar cómo la gravedad la afecta, algo que nunca se ha medido directamente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este descubrimiento para explorar más a fondo las propiedades de la antimateria. Esperan usar haces de positronio para estudiar materiales sin dañarlos y realizar experimentos que podrían revelar cómo la antimateria reacciona a la gravedad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han demostrado que el positronio, un átomo de antimateria, se comporta como una onda, abriendo nuevos caminos para estudiar la antimateria y la física cuántica.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing, intertwined electron and positron represented as two luminous, complementary orbs orbiting a shared center, passing through a delicate, semi-transparent graphene lattice that creates a gentle, wave-like diffraction pattern of light ripples spreading outward, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a harmonious, abstract quantum atmosphere.

articles.title: ‘World models’ are AI’s latest sensation: what are they and what can they do?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00820-5Training AI world models on data about physical environments could improve their real-world capabilities in technologies such as robotics.
articles.content:
Nvidia’s Cosmos is one world model being trained on physics data about real-world environments.Credit: NVIDIA Corporation

An ongoing trend in artificial intelligence could have huge implications for how the technology is used in research.

Machine-learning systems such as large language models (LLMs), which turn prompts into text, images and video, are becoming increasingly sophisticated and continue to make astonishing progress, including in science. But such ‘generative AI’ tools also have limitations. The approach does not always make accurate predictions about the physical world, and could fail at modelling correctly what would happen if a car were to go off the edge of a cliff, for example. This would have implications for developing effective and safe AI-powered robots and self-driving vehicles.

Some researchers, including the computer scientist and AI pioneer Yann LeCun, who founded the firm Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI) Labs in Paris, have turned their attention to a different type of AI tool, developing systems known as ‘world models’ that are trained on real-world data and can embody virtual, interactive and 3D environments.

The approach is attracting huge investment and business interest. AMI Labs — which is taking a radical approach to world models — has raised more than US$1 billion, a record initial infusion of money for a European company. Technology giants such as Google and Nvidia are also developing world models, as are several other start-up companies.

What is a world model?

There are several definitions of what a world model is. In the broadest sense, any neural network trained on data about the real world (or even about some alternative universe) has some sort of model of a world embedded in it. But over the past two years or so, many researchers have begun to use the term to describe AI that can produce a consistent, explorable and often interactive world that is reminiscent of a first-person video game. A world model has to ‘know’ enough about physics that if the user pushes an object off a table then the object will fall down.

World models also provide a more-interactive experience for a user than does generating images of video material from text prompts. For example, Google Deepmind’s world model Genie 3, which the company released in August 2025, uses simple text descriptions to generate photorealistic environments that can be explored in real time.

The AI revolution is coming to robots: how will it change them?

What sort of data are world models trained on?

The companies that build generative AI systems tend to guard their ‘secret sauce’ fiercely. What is known is that interactive world models are trained, in part, using thousands of hours of videos from the real world, as well as with accurate simulations of physical environments that are programmed to observe the laws of physics.

What AI capabilities could world models unlock?

“The more exciting version of a world model is one in which you can take actions,” says Jeff Clune, a computer scientist at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver, Canada. Such an environment could be a safe setting in which to train AI systems that control robots, and could be much faster than letting robots learn by interacting with physical objects, says computer scientist Anastasis Germanidis, co-founder of Runway, a start-up company in New York City that released a world model called GWM-1 in December 2025.

Does AI already have human-level intelligence? The evidence is clear

How will world models benefit researchers?

A world model such as Genie 3 can provide vast numbers of ways to train the software that can power a robot or a self-driving car, says Clune, who contributed to the development of Genie during a contract with Google DeepMind.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant advancement in AI technology—world models—that can simulate real-world physics and environments, enabling safer and more efficient training of robots and autonomous systems. This breakthrough has broad implications for robotics, autonomous vehicles, and AI research, promising tangible benefits for society by improving AI safety and capabilities. The article provides detailed context about the technology, its development, and potential impact, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New AI 'World Models' offer safer, smarter training for robots and self-driving cars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) has made big leaps with tools that create text, images, and videos from simple prompts. But these tools sometimes struggle to understand how things work in the real world, like what happens if a car drives off a cliff. This makes it hard to trust AI in robots or self-driving cars.

**What happened** – Researchers and companies like Nvidia, Google, and AMI Labs are now building AI systems called ‘world models.’ These models learn from real-world videos and physics rules to create virtual, interactive 3D environments. For example, Google’s Genie 3 can make realistic worlds that users can explore and interact with in real time.

**Impact** – World models can teach AI to better understand how objects move and react, making it safer and faster to train robots and autonomous vehicles without risking real accidents. This means robots could learn in virtual spaces before working in the real world. It’s a big step toward smarter and safer AI-powered machines.

**What's next step** – These world models will keep improving and be used more in research and industry. The money invested, like the $1 billion raised by AMI Labs, shows high hopes for this technology. We can expect more advanced AI that learns from virtual worlds to control robots, cars, and other machines in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI world models create real-feeling virtual spaces that help robots and self-driving cars learn safely and work better in the real world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les ‘modèles du monde’ en IA offrent un entraînement plus sûr et plus intelligent aux robots et voitures autonomes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) a beaucoup progressé avec des outils qui créent du texte, des images et des vidéos à partir de simples instructions. Mais ces outils ont parfois du mal à comprendre comment fonctionnent les choses dans le monde réel, par exemple ce qui se passe si une voiture tombe d’une falaise. Cela complique la confiance dans l’IA pour les robots ou les voitures autonomes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs et entreprises comme Nvidia, Google et AMI Labs développent maintenant des systèmes d’IA appelés « modèles du monde ». Ces modèles apprennent à partir de vidéos réelles et des lois de la physique pour créer des environnements virtuels 3D interactifs. Par exemple, Genie 3 de Google peut générer des mondes réalistes que les utilisateurs peuvent explorer en temps réel.

**Impact** – Les modèles du monde peuvent apprendre à l’IA à mieux comprendre comment les objets bougent et réagissent, rendant l’entraînement des robots et véhicules autonomes plus sûr et plus rapide, sans risquer d’accidents réels. Cela permet aux robots d’apprendre dans des espaces virtuels avant d’agir dans le monde réel. C’est une avancée majeure pour des machines intelligentes et sécurisées.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces modèles vont continuer à s’améliorer et être davantage utilisés dans la recherche et l’industrie. Les investissements, comme le milliard de dollars levé par AMI Labs, montrent l’importance de cette technologie. On peut s’attendre à des IA plus avancées qui apprendront dans des mondes virtuels pour piloter robots, voitures et autres machines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les modèles du monde en IA créent des espaces virtuels réalistes qui aident les robots et voitures autonomes à apprendre en toute sécurité et à mieux fonctionner dans le monde réel.
articles.personality_title_es: Los ‘modelos del mundo’ en IA ofrecen entrenamiento más seguro e inteligente para robots y autos autónomos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) ha avanzado mucho con herramientas que crean texto, imágenes y videos a partir de instrucciones simples. Pero estas herramientas a veces tienen dificultades para entender cómo funcionan las cosas en el mundo real, como qué pasa si un auto cae de un acantilado. Esto dificulta confiar en la IA para robots o autos autónomos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores y empresas como Nvidia, Google y AMI Labs están creando sistemas de IA llamados ‘modelos del mundo’. Estos modelos aprenden de videos reales y de las leyes de la física para crear ambientes virtuales 3D interactivos. Por ejemplo, Genie 3 de Google puede generar mundos realistas que los usuarios pueden explorar en tiempo real.

**Impacto** – Los modelos del mundo pueden enseñar a la IA a entender mejor cómo se mueven y reaccionan los objetos, haciendo que el entrenamiento de robots y vehículos autónomos sea más seguro y rápido, sin riesgo de accidentes reales. Esto permite que los robots aprendan en espacios virtuales antes de trabajar en el mundo real. Es un gran avance para máquinas inteligentes y seguras.

**Próximo paso** – Estos modelos seguirán mejorando y usándose más en la investigación y la industria. La inversión, como los 1,000 millones de dólares que AMI Labs ha recaudado, muestra la gran expectativa en esta tecnología. Podemos esperar IA más avanzadas que aprendan en mundos virtuales para controlar robots, autos y otras máquinas.

**Frase resumen** – Los modelos del mundo en IA crean espacios virtuales realistas que ayudan a robots y autos autónomos a aprender de forma segura y a funcionar mejor en el mundo real.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, translucent 3D cube floating above an open hand, inside the cube a miniature interactive virtual world unfolds with tiny falling objects obeying gravity, surrounded by softly swirling streams of data and light representing AI learning from real-world physics, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: Scientists capture electrons forming strange patchy patterns inside quantum materials
articles.summary: Researchers have, for the first time, directly visualized how electronic patterns known as charge density waves evolve across a phase transition. Using cutting-edge microscopy, they found these patterns form unevenly, breaking into patches influenced by tiny structural distortions. Unexpectedly, small pockets of order persist even above the transition temperature. This reveals that electronic order fades gradually rather than disappearing all at once.
articles.content:
In quantum materials, electronic order rarely forms in a smooth, uniform way. Instead, it often appears as complex patterns that vary from one region to another. One well-known example is the charge density wave (CDW), a state in which electrons organize into repeating patterns at low temperatures. While CDWs have been studied for many years, researchers have struggled to directly observe how their strength and spatial coherence change during a phase transition.

A research team led by Professor Yongsoo Yang of the Department of Physics at KAIST (Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology), working with Professors SungBin Lee, Heejun Yang, and Yeongkwan Kim and collaborators at Stanford University, has now achieved a major breakthrough. For the first time, they directly visualized how the amplitude of charge density wave order evolves across space inside a quantum material.

Nanoscale Imaging Reveals Patchy Electron Patterns

To accomplish this, the researchers used a liquid-helium-cooled electron microscope along with four-dimensional scanning transmission electron microscopy (4D-STEM). This advanced setup allowed them to track how CDW order forms, weakens, and breaks apart as temperature changes. More importantly, they were able to create detailed nanoscale maps showing not just whether electronic order is present, but how strong it is and how it connects across different regions.

The process can be compared to watching ice crystals form as water freezes, captured with extremely high magnification. In this case, however, the team observed electrons arranging themselves at temperatures near -253°C. Their microscope could resolve structures as small as one hundred-thousandth the width of a human hair. The images revealed that electronic order does not spread evenly. Some areas showed clear, well-defined patterns, while nearby regions had none at all, resembling a lake where ice forms in scattered patches rather than covering the surface all at once.

Strain and the Breakdown of Electronic Order

The study also found that these uneven patterns are closely tied to tiny distortions within the crystal. Even minute amounts of strain, far too small to detect with conventional optical methods, were enough to significantly weaken the CDW amplitude. This strong link between strain and electronic order provides direct evidence that subtle lattice distortions play a crucial role in shaping how these patterns form.

Another surprising result was the discovery that small pockets of CDW order can persist even above the transition temperature, where long-range order is typically expected to vanish. These isolated regions suggest that the transition is not a simple, uniform process. Instead of disappearing all at once, electronic order gradually loses its spatial coherence.

Measuring How Electronic Order Fades

A key achievement of the work is the first direct measurement of correlations in CDW amplitude. By examining how the strength of electronic order at one location relates to that at another, the researchers showed how coherence breaks down across the transition while local amplitude remains present. This level of detail was not accessible using traditional diffraction or scanning probe techniques.

A New Framework for Understanding Quantum Materials

Charge density waves are a fundamental feature of many quantum materials and often interact with other electronic states. By directly mapping their spatial structure and correlations, this study provides a new experimental approach for understanding how collective electronic order forms and evolves in real systems.

Dr. Yongsoo Yang emphasized the importance of the findings: "Until now, the spatial coherence of charge density waves was largely inferred indirectly. Our approach allows us to directly visualize how electronic order varies across space and temperature, and to identify the factors that locally stabilize or suppress it."

The study, with Seokjo Hong, Jaewhan Oh and Jemin Park of KAIST as co-first authors, was published in Physical Review Letters.

The research was mainly supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grants (Individual Basic Research Program, Basic Research Laboratory Program, Nanomaterial Technology Development Program) funded by the Korean Government (MSIT).

The authors thank E.-G. Moon for helpful discussions. This research was mainly supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grants (RS-2023-00208179 and RS-2025-02243032) funded by the Korean Government (MSIT). Y.Y. also acknowledges the support from the KAIST singularity professor program. S.B.L. was supported by NRF Grant (2021R1A2C109306013) and Nano Material Technology Development Program through the NRF funded by MSIT (RS-2023-00281839). Y.K. was financially supported by NRF Grant (No. RS-2022-00143178 and No. RS-2024-00345856) and Korea Research Institute of Standards and Science (KRISS) (Grant No. KRISS-GP2025-0015). H. Y. was supported by an NRF Grant No. RS-2024-00340377 funded by MSIT. The 4D-STEM, ADF-STEM and EELS experiments were conducted using a double Cs corrected Titan cubed G2 60-300 (FEI) and Spectra Ultra (ThermoFisher) equipment at KAIST Analysis Center for Research Advancement (KARA). Excellent support by Hyung Bin Bae, Jin-Seok Choi and the staff of KARA is gratefully acknowledged. We declare that the authors utilized the ChatGPT for language editing purpose only, and the original manuscript texts were all written by human authors, not by artificial intelligence.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers directly visualized the evolution of charge density waves in quantum materials using advanced microscopy. This discovery provides new insights into electronic order and phase transitions, which is a meaningful advancement in quantum materials research with broad implications for future technology development.
articles.personality_title: Scientists visualize patchy electron patterns inside quantum materials
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum materials are special substances where electrons can organize themselves in unusual ways. One such pattern is called a charge density wave (CDW), where electrons form repeating patterns at very low temperatures. Until now, scientists could not clearly see how these patterns change when the material’s state shifts with temperature.

**What happened** – A team led by Professor Yongsoo Yang at KAIST used powerful, very cold electron microscopes to watch CDW patterns form and fade inside a quantum material. They saw that the patterns do not spread evenly but break into small patches. Tiny strains in the material’s structure affect these patches, and some order remains even when the temperature rises above the usual point where the pattern disappears.

**Impact** – This is the first time researchers have directly mapped how CDW patterns develop and break down on a very small scale. It shows that electronic order fades gradually, rather than vanishing suddenly. Understanding this patchy behavior helps scientists learn more about how electrons act in quantum materials, which could improve future technologies that rely on these materials.

**What's next step** – Researchers can now use this detailed mapping method to study other quantum materials and electronic states. This may lead to better control over electronic properties in new devices, such as faster computers or advanced sensors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have directly observed how electrons form uneven, patchy patterns in quantum materials, revealing new details about how electronic order changes with temperature.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques visualisent des motifs électroniques irréguliers dans des matériaux quantiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les matériaux quantiques sont des substances spéciales où les électrons peuvent s’organiser de manière inhabituelle. Un exemple est la vague de densité de charge (VDC), un motif répétitif formé par les électrons à très basse température. Jusqu’à présent, il était difficile de voir clairement comment ces motifs évoluent lors d’un changement d’état du matériau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par le professeur Yongsoo Yang à KAIST a utilisé des microscopes électroniques très puissants et refroidis pour observer la formation et la disparition des motifs VDC à l’intérieur d’un matériau quantique. Ils ont constaté que les motifs ne se forment pas de manière uniforme mais en petites zones. De petites déformations dans la structure du matériau influencent ces zones, et certains motifs persistent même au-dessus de la température où ils devraient normalement disparaître.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les chercheurs cartographient directement l’évolution des motifs VDC à très petite échelle. Ils montrent que l’ordre électronique s’estompe progressivement au lieu de disparaître brusquement. Comprendre ce comportement en patchs aide à mieux connaître le fonctionnement des électrons dans les matériaux quantiques, ce qui peut améliorer les technologies futures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs pourront utiliser cette méthode de cartographie détaillée pour étudier d’autres matériaux quantiques et états électroniques. Cela pourrait permettre de mieux contrôler les propriétés électroniques dans de nouveaux dispositifs, comme des ordinateurs plus rapides ou des capteurs avancés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont observé directement comment les électrons forment des motifs irréguliers dans des matériaux quantiques, révélant de nouveaux détails sur l’évolution de l’ordre électronique avec la température.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos visualizan patrones electrónicos irregulares dentro de materiales cuánticos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los materiales cuánticos son sustancias especiales donde los electrones pueden organizarse de formas poco comunes. Un ejemplo es la onda de densidad de carga (ODC), un patrón repetitivo que forman los electrones a temperaturas muy bajas. Hasta ahora, era difícil ver claramente cómo estos patrones cambian cuando el estado del material varía con la temperatura.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por el profesor Yongsoo Yang en KAIST utilizó potentes microscopios electrónicos enfriados para observar cómo se forman y desaparecen los patrones ODC dentro de un material cuántico. Descubrieron que los patrones no se extienden de manera uniforme, sino que se dividen en parches pequeños. Pequeñas tensiones en la estructura del material afectan estos parches, y algunos patrones permanecen incluso cuando la temperatura supera el punto donde deberían desaparecer.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que los científicos mapean directamente cómo evolucionan los patrones ODC a escala muy pequeña. Muestran que el orden electrónico se desvanece poco a poco, no de forma repentina. Entender este comportamiento en parches ayuda a conocer mejor cómo actúan los electrones en materiales cuánticos, lo que podría mejorar tecnologías futuras.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores podrán usar este método detallado para estudiar otros materiales cuánticos y estados electrónicos. Esto podría permitir un mejor control de las propiedades electrónicas en nuevos dispositivos, como computadoras más rápidas o sensores avanzados.

**Frase clave** – Científicos observaron directamente cómo los electrones forman patrones irregulares en materiales cuánticos, revelando nuevos detalles sobre cómo cambia el orden electrónico con la temperatura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate icy lake at nanoscale, where shimmering patchy ice crystals form uneven, intricate patterns on the water’s surface, symbolizing electrons arranging into complex charge density waves; subtle ripples and gentle distortions in the water represent tiny strains in the crystal lattice affecting the ice formation, all rendered with soft natural blues, grays, and silvery whites to evoke a calm, scientific wonder.

articles.title: Humanoid robots may be about to break the 100-metre sprint record
articles.summary: Robots can now run a half-marathon faster than humans and are rapidly homing in on the 100-metre sprint record. But why are companies so keen to create speedy robots that have no obvious application in homes or factories?
articles.content:
Robots can now run a half-marathon faster than humans and are rapidly homing in on the 100-metre sprint record. But why are companies so keen to create speedy robots that have no obvious application in homes or factories?

A humanoid robot from Honor crosses the finish line during the 2026 Beijing E-Town Humanoid Robot Half-Marathon Lintao Zhang/Getty Images

Last weekend, Sabastian Sawe set a new world record with a sub-2-hour marathon, but he isn’t the only one raising the bar for runners. On 19 April, a robot from Chinese smartphone maker Honor surpassed the human record for the half-marathon. In another event this month, a robot from Unitree came tantalisingly close to the human 100-metre sprint pace. These developments raise two big questions: how much quicker can humanoid robots get, and what’s the point of fast-running robots anyway?

The inaugural Beijing E-Town Half-Marathon and Humanoid Robot Half-Marathon, where humans and robots competed on the same 21.1-kilometre course, took place in 2025. This month, the second edition saw the number of robotic teams grow nearly fivefold, with more than 100 teams bringing more than 300 humanoid robots to compete. And while the fastest half-marathon time for an autonomous robot in 2025 was 2 hours and 40 minutes, this year that fell dramatically to just over 50 minutes.

Elsewhere, robot-maker Unitree announced that its bipedal H1 model reached a record 10.1 metres per second. For context, Usain Bolt’s record time for the 100-metre distance requires an average speed of 10.44 metres per second – meaning the human record is within grasping distance.

Several factors have contributed to the rapid improvement of running robots in recent years, says Petar Kormushev at Imperial College London. There has been a dramatic reduction in the price of components, but also the emergence of higher-quality components such as stronger, more efficient motors that are quicker to react and move. Computer chips have also become faster and less power-hungry, allowing machines to run much more complex control algorithms. Communications between parts can also be faster, and sensors are smaller and more accurate.

But if speed is the aim, making robots that mimic humans is unlikely to be a winning strategy. “Humans are not that optimised for running because our primary need to survive and to evolve was not running,” says Behnam Dadashzadeh at Bournemouth University, UK. Instead, robots replicating the way an emu runs are up to 300 per cent more efficient than those designed with human-like legs, research suggests.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

Dadashzadeh is unsure that making running robots will directly translate to any benefits in the home or the factory, where humanoid robots are expected to see service. If we need robots to go really fast, then we can simply fit them with wheels, he says.

While there isn’t necessarily a commercial demand for a running robot, these competitions are a good showcase, says Kormushev. “It’s like a stress test for the hardware, because you need to exert high torques on the actuators for a prolonged period of time, which can cause overheating,” he says. “The impact with the ground causes shocks to the gear boxes, and if they’re not good quality, it’s very easy to break teeth on the gears.”

It’s not unlike car-makers entering demanding rallies with rocky roads, jumps and hairpin corners. Popping to the shops won’t be as demanding, but racing reassures customers that the manufacturer knows how to make a resilient product. Neither Unitree nor Honor were available to talk to New Scientist about their motivations.

But competition can also create perverse incentives that lead to designs unsuitable for wider uses. Kormushev says the robots you see in running competitions will be highly specialised, with no working hands or face – sometimes even no head at all – and large hip joints designed purely for moving forwards at speed. “If it has to go sideways it will struggle, and any other movement may be very difficult for it because the distribution of mass and power is optimised only for forward locomotion,” he says.

That’s not to say that humanoid robots will not be useful once they become capable and cheap. A robot that looks like a human would have several advantages in a world designed for humans, such as the capability to master door handles, staircases, furniture and tools.

Just how fast can a humanoid robot go? Dadashzadeh thinks we probably aren’t far off the limit already for robots that look and work like humans. He believes that such robots will beat all human records, but not go much further. “They will be in the same scale, but robots are going to be a bit faster,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological advancement where humanoid robots are rapidly improving their running speeds, even surpassing human half-marathon records and approaching 100-metre sprint records. This represents a meaningful breakthrough in robotics technology with broad implications for hardware resilience, control algorithms, and future robotic applications in human environments. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the potential impact of these innovations beyond niche competitions.
articles.personality_title: Humanoid robots near human sprint records with faster running speeds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Recently, humanoid robots have been improving their running speeds quickly. They can now run a half-marathon faster than humans and are close to matching the fastest human 100-metre sprint times.

**What happened** – In April 2026, a robot made by Chinese company Honor ran a half-marathon in just over 50 minutes, beating the previous robot record of 2 hours and 40 minutes from 2025. Another robot from Unitree nearly matched the human 100-metre sprint speed, reaching 10.1 meters per second compared to Usain Bolt’s 10.44 meters per second.

**Impact** – These advances show big improvements in robot parts like motors, sensors, and computer chips. Racing robots helps test how strong and reliable their parts are under stress, similar to how car makers test vehicles in tough races. Even though fast running robots might not be useful in homes or factories, the technology behind them could make future robots better at moving and handling daily tasks.

**What's next step** – Researchers expect humanoid robots to soon beat human speed records but not by a huge margin. They also believe robots designed to run like other animals, such as emus, could be even faster. Meanwhile, robot makers will keep improving designs to make robots more useful in everyday life, not just in races.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Humanoid robots are quickly closing in on human running speeds, showing important progress in robot technology that could improve future everyday robots.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les robots humanoïdes approchent des records humains de sprint grâce à leur vitesse accrue
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Récemment, les robots humanoïdes améliorent rapidement leur vitesse de course. Ils peuvent désormais courir un semi-marathon plus vite que les humains et s'approchent des records humains du 100 mètres.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En avril 2026, un robot de la société chinoise Honor a couru un semi-marathon en un peu plus de 50 minutes, battant le précédent record robotique de 2 heures 40 minutes établi en 2025. Un autre robot de Unitree s'est presque rapproché de la vitesse humaine du 100 mètres, atteignant 10,1 mètres par seconde contre 10,44 mètres par seconde pour Usain Bolt.

**Impact** – Ces progrès montrent de grandes améliorations dans les pièces des robots comme les moteurs, les capteurs et les puces informatiques. Les courses de robots permettent de tester la solidité de leurs composants sous stress, comme les constructeurs automobiles testent leurs voitures dans des courses difficiles. Même si les robots rapides ne sont pas forcément utiles à la maison ou en usine, cette technologie pourrait rendre les robots futurs meilleurs pour se déplacer et gérer des tâches quotidiennes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs pensent que les robots humanoïdes vont bientôt battre les records humains de vitesse, mais pas de beaucoup. Ils croient aussi que des robots imitant la course d'autres animaux, comme l'émeu, pourraient être encore plus rapides. En attendant, les fabricants continueront à améliorer les robots pour les rendre plus utiles dans la vie de tous les jours, pas seulement dans les compétitions.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les robots humanoïdes se rapprochent rapidement des vitesses de course humaines, montrant des progrès importants dans la technologie robotique qui pourraient améliorer les robots du quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: Los robots humanoides se acercan a los récords humanos de velocidad con carreras más rápidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Recientemente, los robots humanoides han mejorado rápidamente su velocidad al correr. Ahora pueden correr una media maratón más rápido que los humanos y están cerca de igualar los récords humanos en los 100 metros.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, un robot de la empresa china Honor corrió una media maratón en poco más de 50 minutos, superando el récord anterior de robots de 2 horas y 40 minutos de 2025. Otro robot de Unitree casi igualó la velocidad humana en los 100 metros, alcanzando 10.1 metros por segundo frente a los 10.44 metros por segundo de Usain Bolt.

**Impacto** – Estos avances muestran grandes mejoras en las piezas de los robots como motores, sensores y chips informáticos. Las carreras de robots ayudan a probar la fuerza y resistencia de sus componentes bajo presión, parecido a cómo los fabricantes de autos prueban sus vehículos en carreras difíciles. Aunque los robots rápidos no sean útiles en casas o fábricas, esta tecnología podría hacer que los robots del futuro se muevan mejor y realicen tareas diarias.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que pronto los robots humanoides superen los récords humanos de velocidad, pero no por mucho. También creen que los robots que imiten la carrera de otros animales, como el emú, podrían ser aún más rápidos. Mientras tanto, los fabricantes seguirán mejorando los diseños para que los robots sean más útiles en la vida diaria, no solo en competencias.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los robots humanoides se acercan rápidamente a las velocidades humanas al correr, mostrando avances importantes en tecnología robótica que podrían mejorar los robots cotidianos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two sleek humanoid robot silhouettes in dynamic running poses on a minimalist racetrack, their mechanical joints glowing softly with gentle light, set against a softly muted background of a stylized marathon course with subtle hints of a cheering, abstract crowd formed by simple shapes and warm earth tones.
